food_name: Lo Mai Gai (Steamed Glutinous Rice in Lotus Leaf with Chicken)

local_names.malay: Nasi Pulut Ayam Bungkus Daun Teratai
local_names.romanized_local: Lo6 Mai5 Gai1 (Cantonese); Nuò Mǐ Jī (Mandarin)

aliases: Nasi Pulut Ayam Bungkus Daun Teratai

malay: Nasi Pulut Ayam Bungkus Daun Teratai

region_of_origin: Guangdong, China; widely consumed in Singapore and Southeast Asia
food_subgroup: Steamed glutinous rice (sticky rice / pulut) parcel wrapped in lotus leaf with savoury fillings including chicken, Chinese sausage, salted egg yolk, shiitake mushrooms, and dried shrimp

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific term for Lo Mai Gai or steamed glutinous rice dim sum parcels. Closest parent would be a cooked rice dish concept. No code assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Rice-based mixed dish (closest defensible FoodEx2 parent: composite rice dish). No specific FoodEx2 code exists for glutinous rice lotus-leaf parcels; A000E (Composite food — cereal-based) used as closest parent anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No specific FoodEx2 code exists for Lo Mai Gai. A000E used as closest defensible cereal-based composite dish anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept found for Lo Mai Gai or steamed glutinous rice lotus-leaf parcel.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard Lo Mai Gai lotus-leaf parcel weighs approximately 220 g and is typically consumed as a complete breakfast, brunch, or lunch item at yum cha.

nutrient_source.title: Lo Mai Gai (Steamed Glutinous Rice in Lotus Leaf with Chicken) — Health Promotion Board Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) — Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values per 100 g sourced from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo.sg) for Lo Mai Gai. Values represent the composite dish including glutinous rice, chicken, Chinese sausage, salted egg yolk, mushrooms, and dried shrimp fillings. Core macros and micronutrients carried forward from verified input record with HPB provenance.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Glutinous Rice and Rice-Based Products — University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2008; Brand-Miller et al.)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values (Diabetes Care, 2008)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 98 is derived from published GI values for glutinous (waxy) rice, which is composed of >99% amylopectin and has a consistently reported GI of 92–98 in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2008; Brand-Miller JC et al.). Lo Mai Gai is approximately 70% glutinous rice by weight; the protein, fat, and fibre from fillings (chicken, sausage, egg) do not meaningfully attenuate the GI of the dominant amylopectin starch matrix. GL per 220 g parcel = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (98 × 66.0) / 100 = 64.7. Available carbohydrate per serving: 30.0 g/100g × 220g = 66.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content — 295 mg/100 g; 649 mg per 220 g parcel (32.5% of 2,000 mg daily RDI). Sodium load is compounded by Chinese sausage (lap cheong, ~1,000 mg Na/100 g), salted egg yolk, dried shrimp, oyster sauce, and soy sauce.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit Lo Mai Gai to half a parcel (~110 g, ~325 mg sodium) and avoid pairing it with other high-sodium dim sum items such as siu mai or cheung fun with soy sauce at the same meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Extremely high glycemic load — GI ~98, GL ~64.7 per single 220 g parcel. Glutinous rice is composed of >99% amylopectin, the most rapidly digested starch structure, producing a glycemic impact equivalent to approximately two bowls of white rice from a single parcel.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Lo Mai Gai is contraindicated for patients with Type 2 Diabetes unless strictly limited to half a parcel (~110 g, GL ~32); clinicians should explicitly counsel T2DM patients to substitute Lo Mai Gai with lower-GI dim sum options such as har gau (GL ~5.5) or siu mai when attending yum cha.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (2.0 g/100 g; 4.4 g per parcel) from Chinese sausage and egg yolk; high sodium (649 mg per parcel); Chinese sausage (lap cheong) is classified as a processed red meat (IARC Group 1 carcinogen) and contributes to cardiovascular risk through sodium, saturated fat, and nitrate content.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit Lo Mai Gai consumption to occasional intake, request parcels without Chinese sausage where possible, and account for the high sodium contribution within their daily cardiovascular dietary targets.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lo Mai Gai (糯米雞 / 糯米鸡, Cantonese: Lo6 Mai5 Gai1; Mandarin: Nuò Mǐ Jī) is a classic Cantonese dim sum dish originating in Guangdong, China, and widely consumed at yum cha restaurants and Chinese rice shops throughout Singapore and Southeast Asia. The dish consists of glutinous rice (sticky rice / pulut) that has been par-boiled and stir-fried with soy sauce, oyster sauce, and sesame oil, then packed around a savoury filling of marinated chicken, dried shiitake mushrooms, Chinese sausage (lap cheong), salted egg yolk, and dried shrimp. The assembled parcel is wrapped tightly in a large dried lotus leaf and steamed until the rice is fully cooked and fragrant. The lotus leaf imparts a distinctive earthy, floral aroma that permeates the rice during steaming. The texture is dense, moist, and sticky, with a rich umami flavour from the soy-based seasonings and the savoury fillings. A single parcel weighs approximately 220 g and is commonly consumed as a complete breakfast or lunch. Nutritionally, Lo Mai Gai is energy-dense at 210 kcal/100 g (462 kcal per parcel), with 30 g carbohydrate/100 g (66 g per parcel) almost entirely from amylopectin-dominant glutinous rice. The GI is approximately 98 — among the highest of any staple food — producing a glycemic load of ~64.7 per parcel, equivalent to the glycemic impact of two bowls of white rice. This makes Lo Mai Gai the most clinically significant dim sum item for Type 2 Diabetes counselling. The sodium content of 295 mg/100 g (649 mg per parcel) is also clinically relevant for hypertension management. Despite its appearance as a protein-and-rice dish, the metabolic impact is dominated by the rapidly digested glutinous rice starch matrix.
common_user_queries: lo mai gai how many calories

confidence_reason: Core macros and micronutrients sourced from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo.sg), the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. GI value of 98 is consistent with published University of Sydney GI Database values for glutinous rice and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008). Portion size of 220 g is verified. All mandatory multilingual names for a Singaporean dish are present.
notes: Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g/100 g — confirmed negligible/trace per HPB source for this dish type; this is a confirmed zero for the composite dish as prepared. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat values are not reported by the HPB source for this entry and are set to -1. Water content not reported by source; set to -1. Copper (0.1 mg/100g), manganese (0.25 mg/100g), and vitamin K (0.5 mcg/100g) were sourced from enriched_json with confidence 0.9 in the input record; these are retained as supporting micronutrient data but not used as primary evidence for scoring. The GL calculation: available carbohydrate per 220g serving = 30.0 × 2.2 = 66.0 g; GL = (98 × 66.0) / 100 = 64.68, rounded to 64.7. FoodEx2 code A000E is used as the closest defensible parent anchor for a cereal-based composite dish; no specific FoodEx2 code exists for Lo Mai Gai. Halal status is marked false because Chinese sausage (lap cheong) typically contains pork and is not halal-certified in standard preparations.
